<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref path=svl-integration-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00110: SVL Integration VI

- bundle_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `svl-integration-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/svl-integration-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Weighting and Integration VIs Requires: Sound and Vibration Toolkit Converts acceleration to velocity or displacement using a time-domain waveform as the input. Wire data to the scaled signal [EU] input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. Det

### SVL Integration VI

**Owning Palette:**
 Weighting and Integration VIs

**Requires:**
 Sound and Vibration Toolkit

Converts acceleration to velocity or displacement using a time-domain waveform as the input. Wire data to the 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

Details Example

| SVL Integration (1 Ch)SVL Integration (N Ch) |
| --- |

SVL Integration (1 Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Integration_1_Ch.gif']

|  | length units specifies the desired units for length in the integrated spectrum. Use this parameter to change the base units for the integrated spectrum. 0 no change 1 m (default) 2 mm 3 microns 4 in 5 mils |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | no change |
| 1 | m (default) |
| 2 | mm |
| 3 | microns |
| 4 | in |
| 5 | mils |
|  | scaled signal [EU] specifies the scaled signal expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define a virtual channel in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | integration type specifies the type of integration to perform. The default is double integration. 0 Single integration Returns the velocity if the input is acceleration. 1 Double integration (default)Returns the displacement if the input is acceleration. |
| 0 | Single integration Returns the velocity if the input is acceleration. |
| 1 | Double integration (default)Returns the displacement if the input is acceleration. |
|  | highpass cutoff frequency specifies the cutoff frequency for the highpass filter. The default is 1.00. Frequency components less than highpass cutoff frequency are attenuated by the highpass filter. Specify this parameter if you know the lower limit of the frequency response of your transducer. Typically, the frequency response is found on the datasheet for your sensor. For example, if you are using an accelerometer that shows poor response below 20 Hz, set highpass cutoff frequency to 20 Hz . You may also specify a higher highpass cutoff frequency to reduce the settling time of your integrated signals. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | reset filter specifies if the various filters used for the integration have to be reset. The default is FALSE. The filters are automatically reset the first time this VI is called. |
|  | integrated signal returns a single-integrated or double-integrated signal. t0 returns the start time of the waveform. dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 returns the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | unit label returns the selected engineering units. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Integration (N Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Integration_N_Ch.gif']

|  | length units specifies the desired units for length in the integrated spectrum. Use this parameter to change the base units for the integrated spectrum. 0 no change 1 m (default) 2 mm 3 microns 4 in 5 mils |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | no change |
| 1 | m (default) |
| 2 | mm |
| 3 | microns |
| 4 | in |
| 5 | mils |
|  | scaled signals [EU] specifies the scaled signals expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define virtual channels in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | integration type specifies the type of integration to perform. The default is double integration. 0 Single integration Returns the velocity if the input is acceleration. 1 Double integration (default)Returns the displacement if the input is acceleration. |
| 0 | Single integration Returns the velocity if the input is acceleration. |
| 1 | Double integration (default)Returns the displacement if the input is acceleration. |
|  | highpass cutoff frequency specifies the cutoff frequency for the highpass filter. The default is 1.00. Frequency components less than highpass cutoff frequency are attenuated by the highpass filter. Specify this parameter if you know the lower limit of the frequency response of your transducer. Typically, the frequency response is found on the datasheet for your sensor. For example, if you are using an accelerometer that shows poor response below 20 Hz, set highpass cutoff frequency to 20 Hz . You may also specify a higher highpass cutoff frequency to reduce the settling time of your integrated signals. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | reset filter specifies if the various filters used for the integration have to be reset. The default is FALSE. The filters are automatically reset the first time this VI is called. |
|  | integrated signals returns an array of single-integrated or double-integrated signals. t0 returns the start time of the waveform. dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 returns the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | unit labels returns the selected engineering units for each channel. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Integration Details

The SVL Integration VI uses digital filters to integrate the input signal, a lowpass filter with a known rolloff to do the actual integration and a highpass filter to remove the DC component from the signal. If you know the lower limit of the frequency response of your transducer, use the 
 **highpass cutoff frequency**
 parameter.

 
 

 
 
Example

Refer to the Integration (Simulated) VI in the 
 labview\examples\Sound and Vibration\Integration
 directory for an example of using the SVL Integration VI.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/open.gif']
 Open example 
 
 [IMAGE alt='image' src='images/find.gif']
 Find related examples

Weighting and Filtering VIs

## TOPIC 00111: SVL Leq Sound Level VI

- bundle_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `svl-leq-sound-level-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/svl-leq-sound-level-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Sound Level VIs Requires: Sound and Vibration Toolkit Computes the linear averaged, or the equivalent continuous sound level (Leq), of scaled signal [EU] . scaled signal [EU] should have a zero DC component. You can attenuate the DC component with hardware AC-coupling filters and wit

### SVL Leq Sound Level VI

**Owning Palette:**
 Sound Level VIs

**Requires:**
 Sound and Vibration Toolkit

Computes the linear averaged, or the equivalent continuous sound level (Leq), of 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 . 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 should have a zero DC component. You can attenuate the DC component with hardware AC-coupling filters and with software high-pass filters. Wire data to the 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

The Sound Level VIs return respective sound pressure levels in dB rms relative to the reference. By default, a sound pressure level of 20 uPa rms is used as the reference for conversion to dB. In contrast, peak and peak-to-peak sound pressure levels are referenced to 20 uPa pk.

| SVL Leq Sound Level (1 Ch)SVL Leq Sound Level (N Ch) |
| --- |

SVL Leq Sound Level (1 Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Leq_Sound_Level_1_Ch.gif']

|  | scaled signal [EU] specifies the scaled signal expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define a virtual channel in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | Leq sound level [dB EU] returns the equivalent continuous sound pressure level (Leq) expressed in decibels. |
|  | unit label returns the selected engineering units. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Leq Sound Level (N Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Leq_Sound_Level_N_Ch.gif']

|  | scaled signals [EU] specifies the scaled signals expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define virtual channels in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | Leq sound levels [dB EU] returns the equivalent continuous sound pressure levels (Leq) expressed in decibels. |
|  | unit labels returns the selected engineering units for each channel. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Parent topic:

Sound Level VIs

## TOPIC 00112: SVL Limit Testing VI

- bundle_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `svl-limit-testing-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/svl-limit-testing-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Data Evaluation VIs Requires: Sound and Vibration Toolkit Performs limit testing on waveforms, spectra, XY data, identified peaks, or scalar measurements. You can specify an upper and lower boundary of the envelope to define a pass range. This VI uses the defined pass range to test t

### SVL Limit Testing VI

**Owning Palette:**
 Data Evaluation VIs

**Requires:**
 Sound and Vibration Toolkit

Performs limit testing on waveforms, spectra, XY data, identified peaks, or scalar measurements. You can specify an upper and lower boundary of the envelope to define a pass range. This VI uses the defined pass range to test the input signal. VI outputs apply only to the current input signal. You can wire output values to a graph to view the limits, signal, and failures. Wire data to the
 **signal in**
 input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

Use the SVPO Limit Testing VI to perform limit testing on embedded targets that have limited computational resources.

Example

| SVL Limit Testing (Waveform)SVL Limit Testing (Waveform) (Scalar Limits)SVL Limit Testing (Cluster of x0, dx, array)SVL Limit Testing (Cluster of x0, dx, array) (Scalar Limits)SVL Limit Testing (XY)SVL Limit Testing (XY) (Scalar Limits)SVL Limit Testing (Peaks)SVL Limit Testing (Peaks) (Scalar Limits)SVL Limit Testing (Scalar) |
| --- |

SVL Limit Testing (Waveform)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Limit_Testing_Waveform.gif']

|  | signal in specifies the waveform to test to make sure it lies in the envelope bounded by the upper and lower limits. |
| --- | --- |
|  | upper limit specifies the upper boundary of the envelope. The default is Inf. x0 specifies the minimum value of the x-axis. dx specifies the x-axis interval between points in the input data. Y specifies the y-axis values for the limit. |
|  | x0 specifies the minimum value of the x-axis. |
|  | dx specifies the x-axis interval between points in the input data. |
|  | Y specifies the y-axis values for the limit. |
|  | lower limit specifies the lower boundary of the envelope. The default is –Inf. x0 specifies the minimum value of the x-axis. dx specifies the x-axis interval between points in the input data. Y specifies the y-axis values for the limit. |
|  | x0 specifies the minimum value of the x-axis. |
|  | dx specifies the x-axis interval between points in the input data. |
|  | Y specifies the y-axis values for the limit. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | test passed? indicates the result of limit mask testing. If TRUE, the signal is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit and the limit testing passed. If FALSE, the limit testing did not pass. |
|  | test results returns the results of the limit testing at each data point. The VI returns TRUE if the data point is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit . |
|  | output values returns signal in , upper limit , lower limit , and the failures. You can wire this output to a graph to view the values. The first element in the array is signal in . The x0 and dx values in this waveform are changed so that it can be easily plotted with the upper and lower limits. The second element in the array is the failure waveform. The failure waveform contains NaN at points where the limit test passes and contains the input signal where the limit test fails. The third and fourth elements of the array are upper limit and lower limit , respectively. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Limit Testing (Waveform) (Scalar Limits)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Limit_Testing_Waveform_Scalar_Limits.gif']

|  | signal in specifies the waveform to test to make sure it lies in the envelope bounded by the upper and lower limits. |
| --- | --- |
|  | upper limit specifies the upper boundary of the envelope as a single scalar value. |
|  | lower limit specifies the lower boundary of the envelope as a single scalar value. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | test passed? indicates the result of limit mask testing. If TRUE, the signal is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit and the limit testing passed. If FALSE, the limit testing did not pass. |
|  | test results returns the results of the limit testing at each data point. The VI returns TRUE if the data point is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit . |
|  | output values returns signal in , upper limit , lower limit , and the failures. You can wire this output to a graph to view the values. The first element in the array is signal in . The second element in the array is the failure waveform. The failure waveform contains NaN at points where the limit test passes and contains the input signal where the limit test fails. The third and fourth elements of the array are upper limit and lower limit , respectively. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Limit Testing (Cluster of x0, dx, array)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Limit_Testing_Cluster.gif']

|  | signal in specifies the signal to test to make sure it lies in the envelope bounded by the upper and lower limits x0 specifies the minimum value of the x-axis. dx specifies the x-axis interval between points in the input data. array specifies the y-axis values for the signal. |
| --- | --- |
|  | x0 specifies the minimum value of the x-axis. |
|  | dx specifies the x-axis interval between points in the input data. |
|  | array specifies the y-axis values for the signal. |
|  | upper limit specifies the upper boundary of the envelope. The default is Inf. x0 specifies the minimum value of the x-axis. dx specifies the x-axis interval between points in the input data. Y specifies the y-axis values for the limit. |
|  | x0 specifies the minimum value of the x-axis. |
|  | dx specifies the x-axis interval between points in the input data. |
|  | Y specifies the y-axis values for the limit. |
|  | lower limit specifies the lower boundary of the envelope. The default is –Inf. x0 specifies the minimum value of the x-axis. dx specifies the x-axis interval between points in the input data. Y specifies the y-axis values for the limit. |
|  | x0 specifies the minimum value of the x-axis. |
|  | dx specifies the x-axis interval between points in the input data. |
|  | Y specifies the y-axis values for the limit. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | test passed? indicates the result of limit mask testing. If TRUE, the signal is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit and the limit testing passed. If FALSE, the limit testing did not pass. |
|  | test results returns the results of the limit testing at each data point. The VI returns TRUE if the data point is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit . |
|  | output values returns signal in , upper limit , lower limit , and the failures. You can wire this output to a graph to view the values. The first element in the array is signal in . The second element in the array is the failure signal. The failure signal contains NaN at points where the limit test passes and contains the input signal where the limit test fails. The third and fourth elements of the array are upper limit and lower limit , respectively. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Limit Testing (Cluster of x0, dx, array) (Scalar Limits)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Limit_Testing_Cluster_Scalar_Limits.gif']

|  | signal in specifies the signal to test to make sure it lies in the envelope bounded by the upper and lower limits x0 specifies the minimum value of the x-axis. dx specifies the x-axis interval between points in the input data. array specifies the y-axis values for the signal. |
| --- | --- |
|  | x0 specifies the minimum value of the x-axis. |
|  | dx specifies the x-axis interval between points in the input data. |
|  | array specifies the y-axis values for the signal. |
|  | upper limit specifies the upper boundary of the envelope as a single scalar value. |
|  | lower limit specifies the lower boundary of the envelope as a single scalar value. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | test passed? indicates the result of limit mask testing. If TRUE, the signal is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit and the limit testing passed. If FALSE, the limit testing did not pass. |
|  | test results returns the results of the limit testing at each data point. The VI returns TRUE if the data point is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit . |
|  | output values returns signal in , upper limit , lower limit , and the failures. You can wire this output to a graph to view the values. The first element in the array is signal in . The second element in the array is the failure signal. The failure signal contains NaN at points where the limit test passes and contains the input signal where the limit test fails. The third and fourth elements of the array are upper limit and lower limit , respectively. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Limit Testing (XY)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Limit_Testing_XY.gif']

|  | signal in specifies the signal to test to make sure it lies in the envelope bounded by the upper and lower limits. X specifies the x-axis value of signal in . Y specifies the y-axis value of signal in . |
| --- | --- |
|  | X specifies the x-axis value of signal in . |
|  | Y specifies the y-axis value of signal in . |
|  | upper limit specifies the upper boundary of the envelope. X specifies the x-axis values for the limit. The values must match the signal in x-axis values. Y specifies the y-axis values for the limit. |
|  | X specifies the x-axis values for the limit. The values must match the signal in x-axis values. |
|  | Y specifies the y-axis values for the limit. |
|  | lower limit specifies the lower boundary of the envelope. X specifies the x-axis values for the limit. The values must match the signal in x-axis values. Y specifies the y-axis values for the limit. |
|  | X specifies the x-axis values for the limit. The values must match the signal in x-axis values. |
|  | Y specifies the y-axis values for the limit. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | test passed? indicates the result of limit mask testing. If TRUE, the signal is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit and the limit testing passed. If FALSE, the limit testing did not pass. |
|  | test results returns the results of the limit testing at each data point. The VI returns TRUE if the data point is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit . |
|  | output values returns signal in , upper limit , lower limit , and the failures. You can wire this output to a graph to view the values. The first element in the array is signal in . The second element in the array is the failure signal. The failure signal contains NaN at points where the limit test passes and contains the input signal where the limit test fails. The third and fourth elements of the array are upper limit and lower limit , respectively. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Limit Testing (XY) (Scalar Limits)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Limit_Testing_XY_Scalar_Limits.gif']

|  | signal in specifies the signal to test to make sure it lies in the envelope bounded by the upper and lower limits. X specifies the x-axis value of signal in . Y specifies the y-axis value of signal in . |
| --- | --- |
|  | X specifies the x-axis value of signal in . |
|  | Y specifies the y-axis value of signal in . |
|  | upper limit specifies the upper boundary of the envelope as a single scalar value. |
|  | lower limit specifies the lower boundary of the envelope as a single scalar value. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | test passed? indicates the result of limit mask testing. If TRUE, the signal is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit and the limit testing passed. If FALSE, the limit testing did not pass. |
|  | test results returns the results of the limit testing at each data point. The VI returns TRUE if the data point is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit . |
|  | output values returns signal in , upper limit , lower limit , and the failures. You can wire this output to a graph to view the values. The first element in the array is signal in . The second element in the array is the failure signal. The failure signal contains NaN at points where the limit test passes and contains the input signal where the limit test fails. The third and fourth elements of the array are upper limit and lower limit , respectively. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Limit Testing (Peaks)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Limit_Testing_Peaks.gif']

|  | peaks in specifies the peaks to test to make sure they lie in the envelope bounded by the upper and lower limits. freq. or order specifies the frequency or order of the peak. amplitude specifies the amplitude of the peak. |
| --- | --- |
|  | freq. or order specifies the frequency or order of the peak. |
|  | amplitude specifies the amplitude of the peak. |
|  | upper limit specifies the constant value that defines the upper boundary of the envelope. freq. or order specifies the frequency or order of the upper limit. amplitude specifies the amplitude of the upper limit. |
|  | freq. or order specifies the frequency or order of the upper limit. |
|  | amplitude specifies the amplitude of the upper limit. |
|  | lower limit specifies the constant value that defines the lower boundary of the envelope. freq. or order specifies the frequency or order of the lower limit. amplitude specifies the amplitude of the lower limit. |
|  | freq. or order specifies the frequency or order of the lower limit. |
|  | amplitude specifies the amplitude of the lower limit. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | test passed? indicates the result of limit mask testing. If TRUE, the signal is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit and the limit testing passed. If FALSE, the limit testing did not pass. |
|  | test results returns the results of the limit testing at each data point. The VI returns TRUE if the data point is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit . |
|  | output values returns peaks in , upper limit , lower limit , and the failures. You can wire this output to a graph to view the values. The first element in the array is peaks in . The second element in the array is the failure waveform. The failure waveform contains NaN at points where the limit test passes and contains the input signal where the limit test fails. The third and fourth elements of the array are upper limit and lower limit , respectively. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Limit Testing (Peaks) (Scalar Limits)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Limit_Testing_Peaks_Scalar_Limits.gif']

|  | peaks in specifies the peaks to test to make sure they lie in the envelope bounded by the upper and lower limits. freq. or order specifies the frequency or order of the peak. amplitude specifies the amplitude of the peak. |
| --- | --- |
|  | freq. or order specifies the frequency or order of the peak. |
|  | amplitude specifies the amplitude of the peak. |
|  | upper limit specifies the upper boundary of the envelope as a single scalar value. |
|  | lower limit specifies the lower boundary of the envelope as a single scalar value. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | test passed? indicates the result of limit mask testing. If TRUE, the signal is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit and the limit testing passed. If FALSE, the limit testing did not pass. |
|  | test results returns the results of the limit testing at each data point. The VI returns TRUE if the data point is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit . |
|  | output values returns peaks in , upper limit , lower limit , and the failures. You can wire this output to a graph to view the values. The first element in the array is peaks in . The second element in the array is the failure waveform. The failure waveform contains NaN at points where the limit test passes and contains the input signal where the limit test fails. The third and fourth elements of the array are upper limit and lower limit , respectively. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Limit Testing (Scalar)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Limit_Testing_Scalar.gif']

|  | value in specifies the value to test to make sure it lies in the envelope bounded by upper limit and lower limit . |
| --- | --- |
|  | upper limit specifies the upper boundary of the envelope. |
|  | lower limit specifies the lower boundary of the envelope. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | test passed? indicates the result of limit mask testing. If TRUE, value in is less than or equal to upper limit and greater than or equal to lower limit and the limit testing passed. If FALSE, the limit testing did not pass. |
|  | output values returns the input value, the test limits, and the failure. You can wire this output to a graph to view the values. value in returns value in unchanged. failure returns the result of the limit test for value in . failure displays NaN at a point where the limit test passes and contains the input value where the limit test fails. upper limit returns upper limit unchanged. lower limit returns lower limit unchanged. |
|  | value in returns value in unchanged. |
|  | failure returns the result of the limit test for value in . failure displays NaN at a point where the limit test passes and contains the input value where the limit test fails. |
|  | upper limit returns upper limit unchanged. |
|  | lower limit returns lower limit unchanged. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Example

Refer to the Limit Testing (Simulated) VI in the 
 labview\examples\Sound and Vibration\Limit Testing
 directory for an example of using the SVL Limit Testing VI.

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref path=svl-max-min-level-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00113: SVL Max-Min Level VI

- bundle_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `svl-max-min-level-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/svl-max-min-level-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Vibration Level VIs Requires: Sound and Vibration Toolkit Computes the maximum and minimum levels of all previous values and the difference between the maximum and minimum levels of scaled signal [EU] or even-angle signal [EU] . scaled signal [EU] should have a zero DC component. You

### SVL Max-Min Level VI

**Owning Palette:**
 Vibration Level VIs

**Requires:**
 Sound and Vibration Toolkit

Computes the maximum and minimum levels of all previous values and the difference between the maximum and minimum levels of 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 or 
 **even-angle signal [EU]**
 . 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 should have a zero DC component. You can attenuate the DC component with hardware AC-coupling filters and with software high-pass filters. Wire data to the 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

| SVL Max-Min Level (Time Waveform) (1 Ch)SVL Max-Min Level (Time Waveform) N Ch)SVL Max-Min Level (Even Angle) (1 Ch)SVL Max-Min Level (Even Angle) (N Ch) |
| --- |

SVL Max-Min Level (Time Waveform) (1 Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Max_Min_Level_1_Ch.gif']

|  | scaled signal [EU] specifies the scaled signal expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define a virtual channel in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | max level [EU] returns the maximum level in engineering units [EU]. |
|  | min level [EU] returns the minimum level in engineering units [EU]. |
|  | max-min level returns the difference between the maximum and the minimum level in engineering units (EU). |
|  | unit label returns the selected engineering units. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Max-Min Level (Time Waveform) N Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Max_Min_Level_N_Ch.gif']

|  | scaled signals [EU] specifies the scaled signals expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define virtual channels in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | max levels [EU] returns the maximum levels in engineering units [EU]. |
|  | min levels [EU] returns the minimum levels in engineering units [EU]. |
|  | max-min levels returns the differences between the maximum and the minimum levels in engineering units [EU]. |
|  | unit labels returns the selected engineering units for each channel. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Max-Min Level (Even Angle) (1 Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Max_Min_Level_Resampled_1_Ch.gif']

|  | even-angle signal [EU] specifies the signal, in engineering units, resampled at an even-angle interval. r0 specifies the start of the revolution index. dr specifies the revolution interval between two sequential samples. even-angle signal specifies data resampled at even-angle intervals. The samples are synchronous to the rotational speed. |
| --- | --- |
|  | r0 specifies the start of the revolution index. |
|  | dr specifies the revolution interval between two sequential samples. |
|  | even-angle signal specifies data resampled at even-angle intervals. The samples are synchronous to the rotational speed. |
|  | even-angle channel info specifies information about the measurement configuration used to acquire the resampled data. Note You must wire even-angle channel info when performing extended measurements. Do not modify the even-angle channel info values. If you want to view the information, you can create a custom probe. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | max level [EU] returns the maximum level in engineering units [EU]. |
|  | min level [EU] returns the minimum level in engineering units [EU]. |
|  | max-min level returns the difference between the maximum and the minimum level in engineering units (EU). |
|  | unit label returns the selected engineering units. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Max-Min Level (Even Angle) (N Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Max_Min_Level_Resampled_N_Ch.gif']

|  | even-angle signals [EU] specifies the signals, in engineering units, resampled at even-angle intervals. r0 specifies the start of the revolution index. dr specifies the revolution interval between two sequential samples. even-angle signal specifies data resampled at even-angle intervals. The samples are synchronous to the rotational speed. |
| --- | --- |
|  | r0 specifies the start of the revolution index. |
|  | dr specifies the revolution interval between two sequential samples. |
|  | even-angle signal specifies data resampled at even-angle intervals. The samples are synchronous to the rotational speed. |
|  | even-angle channel info specifies information about the measurement configuration this VI uses to acquire the resampled data. Note You must wire even-angle channel info when performing extended measurements. Do not modify the even-angle channel info values. If you want to view the information, you can create a custom probe. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | max levels [EU] returns the maximum levels in engineering units [EU]. |
|  | min levels [EU] returns the minimum levels in engineering units [EU]. |
|  | max-min levels returns the differences between the maximum and the minimum levels in engineering units [EU]. |
|  | unit labels returns the selected engineering units for each channel. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref path=svl-measure-propagation-delay-daqmx-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00114: SVL Measure Propagation Delay (DAQmx) VI

- bundle_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `svl-measure-propagation-delay-daqmx-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/svl-measure-propagation-delay-daqmx-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Calibration (DAQmx) VIs Requires: Sound and Vibration Toolkit Measures the propagation delay of the measurement hardware and the device under test (DUT). You can measure the propagation delay of the device under test by subtracting the propagation delay of the measurement system with

### SVL Measure Propagation Delay (DAQmx) VI

**Owning Palette:**
 Calibration (DAQmx) VIs

**Requires:**
 Sound and Vibration Toolkit

Measures the propagation delay of the measurement hardware and the device under test (DUT). You can measure the propagation delay of the device under test by subtracting the propagation delay of the measurement system without the DUT from the measured propagation delay of the entire system, including the DUT. The propagation delay is returned in number of samples. This VI uses the NI-DAQmx driver.

Details

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Measure_Propagation_Delay_DAQmx.gif']

|  | MLS parameters specifies the generated maximum length sequence (MLS) amplitude. The test signal is an MLS signal with a sample length 10 times the maximum expected propagation delay . amplitude [V peak] specifies the height of the test pulse in volts-peak. The default is 1.40 V peak. |
| --- | --- |
|  | amplitude [V peak] specifies the height of the test pulse in volts-peak. The default is 1.40 V peak. |
|  | smooth pulse parameters specifies the width and height of the test pulse. The test pulse is a single cycle of a sine wave windowed with a Hanning window so that the transitions at the edges of the pulse are smooth. pulse width [samples] specifies the width of the test pulse in number of samples. The default is 20 samples. Note The pulse width and the sampling frequency determine the frequency content of the generated pulse. Verify that the selected coupling does not distort the pulse. Use a narrower pulse width to avoid distortion of the pulse when using AC coupling. amplitude [V peak] specifies the height of the test pulse in volts-peak. The default is 1.40 V peak. |
|  | pulse width [samples] specifies the width of the test pulse in number of samples. The default is 20 samples. Note The pulse width and the sampling frequency determine the frequency content of the generated pulse. Verify that the selected coupling does not distort the pulse. Use a narrower pulse width to avoid distortion of the pulse when using AC coupling. |
|  | amplitude [V peak] specifies the height of the test pulse in volts-peak. The default is 1.40 V peak. |
|  | test signal specifies the test signal used to measure propagation delay [samples] . 0 Smooth pulse (default) 1 MLS |
| 0 | Smooth pulse (default) |
| 1 | MLS |
|  | output configuration specifies the configuration parameters to use for the output channel. AO physical channel specifies the names of the physical channels to use to create virtual channels. The control lists all physical channels on devices and modules installed in the system. max value (10 V) specifies the maximum value of the signal in volts. The default is 5.00 V. min value (-10 V) specifies the minimum value of the signal in volts. The default is –5.00 V. terminal configuration specifies the input terminal configuration of the selected channel. 0 Default The default device terminal configuration. Refer to the device documentation for information about the default terminal configuration. 1 RSE The referenced single-ended (RSE) configuration measures the signal with respect to a ground directly connected to the measurement system ground. 2 NRSE The nonreferenced single-ended (NRSE) configuration measures the signal with respect to a single ground, but the potential may differ from the measurement system ground. 3 Differential The differential configuration has neither of its inputs tied to a fixed reference, such as earth or building ground. 4 Pseudodifferential The pseudodifferential configuration references the positive and negative inputs for the channel to measurement system ground through different impedances. The pseudodifferential terminal configuration measures the difference between the positive and negative inputs. |
|  | AO physical channel specifies the names of the physical channels to use to create virtual channels. The control lists all physical channels on devices and modules installed in the system. |
|  | max value (10 V) specifies the maximum value of the signal in volts. The default is 5.00 V. |
|  | min value (-10 V) specifies the minimum value of the signal in volts. The default is –5.00 V. |
|  | terminal configuration specifies the input terminal configuration of the selected channel. 0 Default The default device terminal configuration. Refer to the device documentation for information about the default terminal configuration. 1 RSE The referenced single-ended (RSE) configuration measures the signal with respect to a ground directly connected to the measurement system ground. 2 NRSE The nonreferenced single-ended (NRSE) configuration measures the signal with respect to a single ground, but the potential may differ from the measurement system ground. 3 Differential The differential configuration has neither of its inputs tied to a fixed reference, such as earth or building ground. 4 Pseudodifferential The pseudodifferential configuration references the positive and negative inputs for the channel to measurement system ground through different impedances. The pseudodifferential terminal configuration measures the difference between the positive and negative inputs. |
| 0 | Default The default device terminal configuration. Refer to the device documentation for information about the default terminal configuration. |
| 1 | RSE The referenced single-ended (RSE) configuration measures the signal with respect to a ground directly connected to the measurement system ground. |
| 2 | NRSE The nonreferenced single-ended (NRSE) configuration measures the signal with respect to a single ground, but the potential may differ from the measurement system ground. |
| 3 | Differential The differential configuration has neither of its inputs tied to a fixed reference, such as earth or building ground. |
| 4 | Pseudodifferential The pseudodifferential configuration references the positive and negative inputs for the channel to measurement system ground through different impedances. The pseudodifferential terminal configuration measures the difference between the positive and negative inputs. |
|  | input configuration specifies the configuration parameters to use for the input channel. AI physical channel specifies the names of the physical channels to use to create virtual channels. The control lists all physical channels on devices and modules installed in the system. max value (10 V) specifies the maximum value of the signal in volts. The default is 5.00 V. min value (-10 V) specifies the minimum value of the signal in volts. The default is –5.00 V. terminal configuration specifies the input terminal configuration of the selected channel. 0 Default The default device terminal configuration. Refer to the device documentation for information about the default terminal configuration. 1 RSE The referenced single-ended (RSE) configuration measures the signal with respect to a ground directly connected to the measurement system ground. 2 NRSE The nonreferenced single-ended (NRSE) configuration measures the signal with respect to a single ground, but the potential may differ from the measurement system ground. 3 Differential The differential configuration has neither of its inputs tied to a fixed reference, such as earth or building ground. 4 Pseudodifferential The pseudodifferential configuration references the positive and negative inputs for the channel to measurement system ground through different impedances. The pseudodifferential terminal configuration measures the difference between the positive and negative inputs. DSA channel settings specifies the input coupling and integrated electronic piezoelectric (IEPE) excitation applied if the measurement is performed with a DSA device. coupling specifies the coupling setting of the selected channel. 0 AC 1 DC 2 GND enable IEPE excitation specifies whether the onboard current excitation is enabled to power integrated electronic piezoelectric (IEPE) sensors. The default is FALSE, meaning that the onboard excitation is disabled. IEPE current [mA] specifies the current value in mA for the onboard integrated electronic piezoelectric (IEPE) current excitation. The default is 4.0 mA. |
|  | AI physical channel specifies the names of the physical channels to use to create virtual channels. The control lists all physical channels on devices and modules installed in the system. |
|  | max value (10 V) specifies the maximum value of the signal in volts. The default is 5.00 V. |
|  | min value (-10 V) specifies the minimum value of the signal in volts. The default is –5.00 V. |
|  | terminal configuration specifies the input terminal configuration of the selected channel. 0 Default The default device terminal configuration. Refer to the device documentation for information about the default terminal configuration. 1 RSE The referenced single-ended (RSE) configuration measures the signal with respect to a ground directly connected to the measurement system ground. 2 NRSE The nonreferenced single-ended (NRSE) configuration measures the signal with respect to a single ground, but the potential may differ from the measurement system ground. 3 Differential The differential configuration has neither of its inputs tied to a fixed reference, such as earth or building ground. 4 Pseudodifferential The pseudodifferential configuration references the positive and negative inputs for the channel to measurement system ground through different impedances. The pseudodifferential terminal configuration measures the difference between the positive and negative inputs. |
| 0 | Default The default device terminal configuration. Refer to the device documentation for information about the default terminal configuration. |
| 1 | RSE The referenced single-ended (RSE) configuration measures the signal with respect to a ground directly connected to the measurement system ground. |
| 2 | NRSE The nonreferenced single-ended (NRSE) configuration measures the signal with respect to a single ground, but the potential may differ from the measurement system ground. |
| 3 | Differential The differential configuration has neither of its inputs tied to a fixed reference, such as earth or building ground. |
| 4 | Pseudodifferential The pseudodifferential configuration references the positive and negative inputs for the channel to measurement system ground through different impedances. The pseudodifferential terminal configuration measures the difference between the positive and negative inputs. |
|  | DSA channel settings specifies the input coupling and integrated electronic piezoelectric (IEPE) excitation applied if the measurement is performed with a DSA device. coupling specifies the coupling setting of the selected channel. 0 AC 1 DC 2 GND enable IEPE excitation specifies whether the onboard current excitation is enabled to power integrated electronic piezoelectric (IEPE) sensors. The default is FALSE, meaning that the onboard excitation is disabled. IEPE current [mA] specifies the current value in mA for the onboard integrated electronic piezoelectric (IEPE) current excitation. The default is 4.0 mA. |
|  | coupling specifies the coupling setting of the selected channel. 0 AC 1 DC 2 GND |
| 0 | AC |
| 1 | DC |
| 2 | GND |
|  | enable IEPE excitation specifies whether the onboard current excitation is enabled to power integrated electronic piezoelectric (IEPE) sensors. The default is FALSE, meaning that the onboard excitation is disabled. |
|  | IEPE current [mA] specifies the current value in mA for the onboard integrated electronic piezoelectric (IEPE) current excitation. The default is 4.0 mA. |
|  | maximum propagation delay [samples] specifies the maximum expected propagation delay for each iteration in number of samples. The default is 150 samples. |
|  | number of averages specifies the number of measurements used to compute the average propagation delay [samples] . The default is 10. |
|  | sample frequency [Hz] specifies the update rate to use on the output channel and the sampling rate to use on the input channel in units of samples per second. The default is 50,000 Hz. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | generated and acquired waveforms returns the test signal generated on the output channel and the signal acquired on the input channel. |
|  | average propagation delay [samples] returns the average value in propagation delay [samples] rounded up to the nearest sample. |
|  | propagation delay returns the propagation delay computed for each iteration. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Measure Propagation Delay (DAQmx) Details

Use a smooth pulse to measure the propagation delay of electrical systems. Use MLS when measuring acoustic delays.

Parent topic:

Calibration (DAQmx) VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref path=svl-peak-level-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00115: SVL Peak Level VI

- bundle_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `svl-peak-level-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/svl-peak-level-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Vibration Level VIs Requires: Sound and Vibration Toolkit Computes the peak level of scaled signal [EU] or even-angle signal [EU] . scaled signal [EU] should have a zero DC component. You can attenuate the DC component with hardware AC-coupling filters and with software high-pass fil

### SVL Peak Level VI

**Owning Palette:**
 Vibration Level VIs

**Requires:**
 Sound and Vibration Toolkit

Computes the peak level of 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 or 
 **even-angle signal [EU]**
 . 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 should have a zero DC component. You can attenuate the DC component with hardware AC-coupling filters and with software high-pass filters. The peak level is the maximum of the absolute values of all measured values. Wire data to the 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

| SVL Peak Level (Time Waveform) (1 Ch)SVL Peak Level (Time Waveform) (N Ch)SVL Peak Level (Even Angle) (1 Ch)SVL Peak Level (Even Angle) (N Ch)SVPO Peak Level (1 Ch DBL Array)SVPO Peak Level (N Ch DBL Array)SVPO Peak Level (1 Ch DBL WDT)SVPO Peak Level (N Ch DBL WDT) |
| --- |

SVL Peak Level (Time Waveform) (1 Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Peak_Level_1_Ch.gif']

|  | scaled signal [EU] specifies the scaled signal expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define a virtual channel in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | peak level returns the peak level in engineering units. |
|  | unit label returns the selected engineering units. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |
|  | scaled signals [EU] returns the scaled signals expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define a virtual channel in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 returns the start time of the waveform. dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 returns the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | even-angle channel info specifies information about the measurement configuration used to acquire the resampled data. Note You must wire even-angle channel info when performing extended measurements. Do not modify the even-angle channel info values. If you want to view the information, you can create a custom probe. |

SVL Peak Level (Time Waveform) (N Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Peak_Level_N_Ch.gif']

|  | scaled signals [EU] specifies the scaled signals expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define virtual channels in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | peak levels returns the peak level for each channel in engineering units. |
|  | unit labels returns the selected engineering units for each channel. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |
|  | scaled signals [EU] returns the scaled signals expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define a virtual channel in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 returns the start time of the waveform. dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 returns the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | even-angle channel info specifies information about the measurement configuration used to acquire the resampled data. Note You must wire even-angle channel info when performing extended measurements. Do not modify the even-angle channel info values. If you want to view the information, you can create a custom probe. |

SVL Peak Level (Even Angle) (1 Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Peak_Level_Resampled_1_Ch.gif']

|  | even-angle signal [EU] specifies the signal, in engineering units, resampled at an even-angle interval. r0 specifies the start of the revolution index. dr specifies the revolution interval between two sequential samples. even-angle signal specifies data resampled at even-angle intervals. The samples are synchronous to the rotational speed. |
| --- | --- |
|  | r0 specifies the start of the revolution index. |
|  | dr specifies the revolution interval between two sequential samples. |
|  | even-angle signal specifies data resampled at even-angle intervals. The samples are synchronous to the rotational speed. |
|  | even-angle channel info specifies information about the measurement configuration used to acquire the resampled data. Note You must wire even-angle channel info when performing extended measurements. Do not modify the even-angle channel info values. If you want to view the information, you can create a custom probe. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |

|  | unit label returns the selected engineering units. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |
|  | scaled signals [EU] returns the scaled signals expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define a virtual channel in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 returns the start time of the waveform. dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 returns the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | even-angle channel info specifies information about the measurement configuration used to acquire the resampled data. Note You must wire even-angle channel info when performing extended measurements. Do not modify the even-angle channel info values. If you want to view the information, you can create a custom probe. |

|  | even-angle signals [EU] specifies the signals, in engineering units, resampled at even-angle intervals. r0 specifies the start of the revolution index. dr specifies the revolution interval between two sequential samples. even-angle signal specifies data resampled at even-angle intervals. The samples are synchronous to the rotational speed. |

If you want to view the information, you can create a custom probe. |

|  | min level [EU] returns the minimum level in engineering units [EU]. |
|  | duration [s] returns the time duration since the RMS level measurement starts or since you restart the averaging. |

## TOPIC 00116: SVL Peak Sound Level VI

- bundle_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `svl-peak-sound-level-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/svl-peak-sound-level-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Sound Level VIs Requires: Sound and Vibration Toolkit Computes the peak sound level of scaled signal [EU] . scaled signal [EU] should have a zero DC component. You can attenuate the DC component with hardware AC-coupling filters and with software high-pass filters. The peak sound lev

### SVL Peak Sound Level VI

**Owning Palette:**
 Sound Level VIs

**Requires:**
 Sound and Vibration Toolkit

Computes the peak sound level of 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 . 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 should have a zero DC component. You can attenuate the DC component with hardware AC-coupling filters and with software high-pass filters. The peak sound level is the maximum of the absolute values of all measured values. Wire data to the 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

The Sound Level VIs return respective sound pressure levels in dB rms relative to the reference. By default, a sound pressure level of 20 uPa rms is used as the reference for conversion to dB. In contrast, peak and peak-to-peak sound pressure levels are referenced to 20 uPa pk.

| SVL Peak Sound Level (1 Ch)SVL Peak Sound Level (N Ch) |
| --- |

SVL Peak Sound Level (1 Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Peak_Sound_Level_1_Ch.gif']

|  | scaled signal [EU] specifies the scaled signal expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define a virtual channel in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | peak sound level [dB pk EU] returns the peak sound level expressed in decibels. |
|  | unit label returns the selected engineering units. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL Peak Sound Level (N Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_Peak_Sound_Level_N_Ch.gif']

|  | scaled signals [EU] specifies the scaled signals expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define virtual channels in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | peak sound levels [dB pk EU] returns the peak sound level expressed in decibels. |
|  | unit labels returns the selected engineering units for each channel. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Sound Level VIs

## TOPIC 00117: SVL RMS Level VI

- bundle_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `svl-rms-level-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/svl-rms-level-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Vibration Level VIs Requires: Sound and Vibration Toolkit Computes the root-mean-square (RMS) level of scaled signal [EU] or even-angle signal [EU] . Scaled signal [EU] should have a zero DC component. You can attenuate the DC component with hardware AC-coupling filters and with soft

### SVL RMS Level VI

**Owning Palette:**
 Vibration Level VIs

**Requires:**
 Sound and Vibration Toolkit

Computes the root-mean-square (RMS) level of 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 or 
 **even-angle signal [EU]**
 . 
 **Scaled signal [EU]**
 should have a zero DC component. You can attenuate the DC component with hardware AC-coupling filters and with software high-pass filters. Wire data to the 
 **scaled signal [EU]**
 input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

Use the SVPO RMS Level VI to compute the RMS level on embedded targets that have limited computational resources.

| SVL RMS Level (Time Waveform) (1 Ch)SVL RMS Level (Time Waveform) (N Ch)SVL RMS Level (Even Angle) (1 Ch)SVL RMS Level (Even Angle) (N Ch) |
| --- |

SVL RMS Level (Time Waveform) (1 Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_RMS_Level_1_Ch.gif']

|  | scaled signal [EU] specifies the scaled signal expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define a virtual channel in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | RMS level returns the RMS level in engineering units (EU rms). |
|  | unit label returns the selected engineering units. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL RMS Level (Time Waveform) (N Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_RMS_Level_N_Ch.gif']

|  | scaled signals [EU] specifies the scaled signals expressed in the selected engineering units. To obtain a properly scaled signal, you can use the SVL Scale Voltage to EU VI or define virtual channels in National Instruments Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX). t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval in seconds between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | RMS levels [EU rms] returns the RMS level for each channel in engineering units (EU rms). |
|  | unit labels returns the selected engineering units for each channel. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL RMS Level (Even Angle) (1 Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_RMS_Level_Resampled_1_Ch.gif']

|  | even-angle signal [EU] specifies the signal, in engineering units, resampled at an even-angle interval. r0 specifies the start of the revolution index. dr specifies the revolution interval between two sequential samples. even-angle signal specifies data resampled at even-angle intervals. The samples are synchronous to the rotational speed. |
| --- | --- |
|  | r0 specifies the start of the revolution index. |
|  | dr specifies the revolution interval between two sequential samples. |
|  | even-angle signal specifies data resampled at even-angle intervals. The samples are synchronous to the rotational speed. |
|  | even-angle channel info specifies information about the measurement configuration used to acquire the resampled data. Note You must wire even-angle channel info when performing extended measurements. Do not modify the even-angle channel info values. If you want to view the information, you can create a custom probe. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | restart averaging specifies if the selected averaging process has to be restarted. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the averaging process restarts automatically. |
|  | RMS level returns the RMS level in engineering units (EU rms). |
|  | unit label returns the selected engineering units. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

SVL RMS Level (Even Angle) (N Ch)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/SVL_RMS_Level_Resampled_N_Ch.gif']

|  | even-angle signals [EU] specifies the signals, in engineering units, resampled at even-angle intervals. r0 specifies the start of the revolution index. dr specifies the revolution interval between two sequential samples. even-angle signal specifies data resampled at even-angle intervals. The samples are synchronous to the rotational speed. |
| --- | --- |
|  | r0 specifies the start of the revolution index. |
|  | dr specifies the revolution interval between two sequential samples. |
|  | even-angle signal specifies data resampled at even-angle intervals. The samples are synchronous to the rotational speed. |
|  | even-angle channel info specifies information about the measurement configuration this VI uses to acquire the resampled data. Note You must wire even-angle channel info when performing extended measurements. Do not modify the even-angle channel info values. If you want to view the information, you can create a custom probe. |

## TOPIC 00118: SVL Running Leq Sound Level VI

- bundle_id: `labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `svl-running-leq-sound-level-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-sound-and-vibration-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/svl-running-leq-sound-level-vi.html
